MonitorTest flagged node-not-ready during intentional commatrix nftables reboot on SNO.
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

PROJECT TESTS PASSED — failure is from CI framework


Test 1: [single-node-e2e-test] MonitorTest failure

• Duration: 2h0m46s
• Error: Suite run returned error: failed due to a MonitorTest failure
• Evidence:

```

Suite run returned error: failed due to a MonitorTest failure

error: error running a test suite: failed due to a MonitorTest failure

{"component":"entrypoint","error":"wrapped process failed: exit status 1",...,"msg":"Error executing test process","time":"2026-06-22T15:56:16Z"}

INFO[2026-06-22T15:56:17Z] Step aws-ovn-network-flow-matrix-single-node-single-node-e2e-test failed after 2h0m46s.

```

Commatrix test step confirmed passing beforehand:

```

INFO[2026-06-22T13:55:30Z] Step aws-ovn-network-flow-matrix-single-node-network-flow-matrix-tests succeeded after 9m35s.

{"timestamp":1782136529,"passed":true,"result":"SUCCESS","revision":"main"}

```

Node reboot triggered by commatrix nftables test at 13:50:

```

2026/06/22 13:50:22 Node ip-10-0-20-68.us-west-1.compute.internal is NotReady

STEP: Rebooting first node: ip-10-0-20-68.us-west-1.compute.internal and waiting for disconnect @ 06/22/26 13:50:04.659

```

MonitorTest interval collection spanned this window (13:17:35–15:54:10), catching the reboot-induced NotReady:

```

Processing monitorTest: apiserver-external-availability

finalIntervals size = 120703

first interval time: From = 2026-06-22 13:17:35 +0000 UTC

last interval time: From = 2026-06-22 15:54:10.245733205 +0000 UTC

```

• Root Cause: The commatrix nftables test (nftables_test.go) intentionally applies a MachineConfig to the SNO node and reboots it as part of verifying firewall rules after reboot. The node ip-10-0-20-68.us-west-1.compute.internal went NotReady at 13:50:22. The single-node-e2e-test CI framework step's openshift-tests suite was running MonitorTests (including apiserver-external-availability) that recorded the API server disruption during this intentional reboot and treated it as a test failure. The commatrix project's own 3 tests all passed and its finished.json reports "result":"SUCCESS".
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — this is a CI framework sensitivity issue, not a commatrix regression.
• Source File: https://github.com/openshift-kni/commatrix/blob/release-4.21/test/e2e/nftables_test.go (line ~85, should apply firewall by blocking all ports except the ones OCP is listening on)
• Is it a flake? yes — the SNO reboot is intentional and deterministic; MonitorTest detecting node-not-ready during a deliberate reboot is a recurring pattern in SNO e2e jobs.
• Suggested Fix: Annotate or exempt the node-not-ready interval caused by the MachineConfig-driven reboot in the MonitorTest framework (e.g. add a disruption annotation before/after reboot, similar to how upgrade tests exempt planned reboots). No commatrix code change is needed.


TL;DR: MonitorTest flagged node-not-ready during intentional commatrix nftables reboot on SNO.


Relation Between Failures: There is exactly one failure chain: the commatrix nftables test intentionally rebooted the SNO node via MachineConfig to verify firewall rules, which caused a brief API server disruption; the CI framework's openshift-tests MonitorTest (apiserver-external-availability) captured this as a failure condition and caused the single-node-e2e-test step to exit 1. The commatrix project tests themselves all passed with SUCCESS.


Per-Version Notes:

• 4.21: Confirmed — MonitorTest failure on intentional SNO reboot in single-node-e2e-test step; commatrix tests passed.
• 4.22: Same root cause expected — identical job structure (aws-ovn-network-flow-matrix-single-node) with same nftables reboot step and same single-node-e2e-test MonitorTest wrapper.
• 5.0: Same root cause expected — same job topology; MonitorTest sensitivity to node-not-ready during planned reboots is a CI framework behavior not tied to OCP version.

Affected Images: ci/telco-runner:latest (test runner), registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.21.0-0.nightly-2026-06-21-232046 (cluster under test). No image pull failures observed; all pipeline images tagged successfully.


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: LOW

2026-06-20T06:00

❌ failure4.204.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp209mtest_regression` (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + `infra_other` (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry) CRITICAL
`linuxptp-daemon` PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.
24 failed tests
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.

• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.

• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.

2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.

• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.

Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.

GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma


Cross-Suite CI Failure Summary

Job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp

Version: 4.20 | Total failures: 24/24



TL;DR: linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (merged 2026-06-23) silenced container-log output, crashing log-scraping across 5 suites; API credential expiry collapsed the OC suite entirely.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (upstream-sync-2026-06-22, merged 2026-06-23) broke container-log output (bc: 3, dualfollower: 3, dualnicbc: 3, dualnicbcha: 2, oc: 1)
• PR #613 introduced two simultaneous regressions:
1. Log routing change: When LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true (set because EnableEventPublisher=true in all test clusters), processWithParser() feeds ptp4l output to the cloud-event-proxy socket and returns early — ptp4l/phc2sys lines never reach container stdout. The test XML confirms Original EnableEventPublisher value: true.
2. Log format change: The new printNodeProfile() function logs the raw nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf field, which for test-generated configs starts with [global], not #profile: . The GetProfileLogID regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)*?message_tag \[…\]) therefore never matches even if logs were reachable.
• Both changes cause GetProfileLogID (3-minute timeout) to find nothing and error with could not find regex in log stream … context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. Every ClockSync test in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha hits this path. The oc suite's earliest failure is identical before the auth collapse begins.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server credentials expired mid-run (oc: 10)
• The kubeconfig token mounted at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig expired (or the kube-apiserver VIP 10.8.34.117:6443 was briefly unreachable) during the OC suite's 3598-second window. All 10 tests fail at the first API call with Unauthorized; one test reports connection refused. Post-suite gather steps (gather-must-gather, gather-extra, gather-audit-logs) all independently fail with Unauthorized, confirming cluster-wide credential invalidation, not a test-code issue.

• 2/24 failures — T-GM hardware does not enter holdover (clock class 7) after GNSS loss (tgm: 2)
• Both WPC T-GM holdover tests observe clock class 248 (FREERUN) throughout, never class 7 (HOLDOVER). The DPLL state machine either bypasses DPLL_HOLDOVER and goes directly to DPLL_FREERUN on GNSS loss, or d.inSpec is false, both of which force the class-248 path. The identical outcome for both cable-pull and GNSS-power-cycle tests in the same run suggests a systematic hardware state issue rather than a race, but a re-run is needed to distinguish a regression from a transient hardware condition.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root Causebcdualfollowerdualnicbcdualnicbchaoctgm
PR #613 log routing / format regression33321—
API credential expiry————10—
T-GM holdover hardware failure—————2

• bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha share a single root cause (PR #613) and are not independently broken — fixing PR #613's log-routing behavior would likely unblock all four suites simultaneously.
• oc is partially affected by PR #613 (1 test) but dominated by credential expiry (10 tests) — a separate infrastructure problem.
• tgm is entirely isolated: its failures are hardware/DPLL-state dependent and unrelated to the log-routing regression.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or patch linuxptp-daemon PR #613's log routing change (fixes 12 failures). The test framework reads container logs via kubectl logs; when LOGS_TO_SOCKET=true, ptp4l output is silenced from that stream. Either (a) keep a copy of ptp4l output on container stdout even when the socket is active, or (b) update GetProfileLogID / GetPodLogsRegex in the test helpers to read from the cloud-event-proxy event stream instead of the container log. The regex also needs updating: printNodeProfile now emits the raw Ptp4lConf starting with [global], not #profile: , so the current regex can never match.

2. Investigate and extend the OC suite kubeconfig token lifetime or implement token refresh (fixes 10 failures). The bare-metal lab cluster token expires within the ~3600-second OC suite window. Options: use a service account token with a longer TTL, implement kubeconfig refresh in the test runner, or split the OC suite to run in a shorter window. Also confirm whether the connection refused on 10.8.34.117:6443 was a scheduled kube-apiserver restart or an outage, and gate that cluster for CI if unstable.

3. Re-run the tgm suite in isolation to determine flake vs. regression (affects 2 failures). If the T-GM holdover tests fail a second time, collect DPLL sysfs/netlink state (dpll subsystem, E810 card) at the moment of GNSS loss to confirm whether the hardware DPLL is asserting DPLL_HOLDOVER at all. If it is not, a firmware or kernel driver update may be needed. If the tests pass on re-run, treat as a transient hardware state issue and add a retry annotation.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression (primary, 12/24 failures from PR #613) + infra_other (secondary, 10/24 from credential expiry)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire job failed (24/24 tests); the dominant cause is a code change merged the day before the run that systematically disables the test framework's log-scraping mechanism across all PTP sync suites.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests timeout because the test framework cannot find the message_tag log anchor in a 3+ hour old linuxptp-daemon pod, causing the entire log-scraping chain for foreign clock ID to fail before any PTP sync verification is attempted.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Log-buffer eviction / log-scraping timeout in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 failing tests)
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the build log, the worker nodes show AGE=3h32m at 05:00 UTC, meaning linuxptp-daemon pods have been running for ~3.5 hours by the time the ClockSync tests execute at ~07:02 UTC.


• Root Cause: All 3 tests call BasicClockSyncCheck → ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign → ptphelper.GetProfileLogID. GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:69) searches pod logs for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.*)\] using GetPodLogsRegex with a 3-minute timeout (pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes). GetPodLogsRegex (test/pkg/pods/pods.go:214) first attempts a no-follow read of all existing logs (1-minute snapCtx), then falls back to following the live stream. The Ptp4lConf: #profile: entry is only logged by printNodeProfile at daemon startup (daemon.go:1369) when applyNodePtpProfile writes the config file. After 3.5 hours of ptp4l logging at ~1 Hz (offset, master offset, clock state lines), this initial profile-dump entry has either been evicted from the Kubernetes 10 MB pod log buffer or the follow stream never re-emits it — so neither pass of GetPodLogsRegex finds the anchor, causing the 3-minute timeout and the could not get any profile line error.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no single commit broke this; the log-eviction condition is a latent fragility. The most recent related commits are 3586177 ci: add ginkgo flake mitigation (Ma
